prompt: For returns to physical stores in Tennessee in 2000, find customers whose total returns for a store were more than 1.2 times what the average customer returns for that store were in that period under the same conditions. Print one row per alphanumeric customer id and store_id combo that meet the criteria (so ensure a customer with two stores shows up as a duplicate), but do not show the store_id in results; order by customer id; limit 100 rows.

For web and catalog sales for each calendar week sequence we want the ratio of each day-of-week's sales to the same day-of-week's sales in the next year, as eight values per row: first the calendar week-sequence number itself, then the seven day-of-week ratios (Sunday through Saturday). Each week sequence totals ALL of its days' sales (a week may span multiple years; do not split by year). Each ratio row has  each day-of-week sales divided by the same day-of-week sales in the week sequence 53 weeks in the future, rounded to two decimal places. Order by week sequence asc, only show in output week sequences that ever occur in 2001. (though ratios compare to ~2002 ranges)

For all items from manufacturer 128, looking only at store sales that occurred in the 11th month of the year, report the total extended store sales price grouped by brand and year. Output exactly these columns in this left-to-right order: the year, the numeric brand id, the brand name, and the total extended store sales price. Order the rows by year ascending, then total extended sales price descending, then numeric brand id ascending; limit to 100 rows.

Identify billing customers who generated positive value through all three sales channels — store, catalog, and web in both 2001 and 2002. A customer's annual value in a channel for a year is the sum, over that channel's sales lines in that year, of each line's extended list price minus its extended wholesale cost minus its extended discount amount, plus its extended sales price. A channel's rate of growth from 2001 to 2002 is that customer's 2002 annual value divided by their 2001 annual value. Return up to 100 customers whose year-over-year growth from 2001 to 2002 was stronger in the catalog channel than in both the store channel and the web channel. For each qualifying customer, report: the Customer ID (the stable business identifier, not the internal surrogate key), First Name, Last Name, and Preferred Customer Flag (as recorded on their 2002 store activity). A customer qualifies only if: they have activity in store, catalog, and web sales during both 2001 and 2002; their annual value is positive in 2001 for each of the three channels; their rate of growth from 2001 to 2002 in the catalog channel exceeds their rate of growth over the same period in the store channel; and their rate of growth from 2001 to 2002 in the catalog channel also exceeds their rate of growth over the same period in the web channel. Sort the results by Customer ID, then First Name, Last Name, and Preferred Customer Flag, all ascending with nulls first.

Report output channel entity performance by three metrics and two dimensions in rollup form as five columns in this order: (1) the channel type + channel ('web channel', 'store channel', 'catalog channel'); (2) the entity identifier prefixed by channel with one of 'store' (store channel), 'catalog_page' (catalog channel), or 'web_site' (web channel) (no space; raw concatenation, ex `storeABCDEF`, `catalog_pageABCDEF`, `web_siteABCDEF`; completely null when rolled up); (3) total ext sales; (4) total returns; (5) net profit after subtracting return-related net losses. The entity id for the final result is the return entity id for returns and the sale entity id for sales, merged into single column for aggregate results. Sales individually filtered by sales date between August 23, 2000 and September 6, 2000 and associated with non-null sale entity identifier; returns individually by return date in that same period and use a non-null return store identifier by grouping. An entity ID should appear if it had any sales OR any returns in the period (so an entity with only returns in filter set and no sales still appears; entities with no activity in the period are excluded. Report any empty gross sales, returns, or net profit as 0 rather than null. (rollup format reminder: subtotal rows by channel and a grand total row across all channels). Sort ONLY by the post-group output by channel and entity identifier, nulls first for both! Return only the first 100 rows. An entity may show in both sales or returns or only one for the period; the channel/entity label must be populated even if it is only in returns or sales.

Find the states with at least 10 qualifying line items (a line item being one ticket + item combination) in January 2001 where the item current price is more than 1.2 times the average current price across the distinct items in the same category (use the item level pricing, not the sale price for the item). Consider only items whose category is assigned, and only customers who have a current home address on record. That address may have no state recorded — still include those customers and report them under a null state. Use the state from the customer's own current home address. For states meeting the line item cut off, return the state and that line-item count, ordered by the count ascending with nulls first, then state with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in the year 2000, report the average quantity, average list price, average coupon amount, and average sales price for each item. Filter by the customer-demographic profile recorded on the sale itself (the one linked directly to the sale, NOT the customer's current demographic profile reached through the customer chain): male, single, 'College' education. Also restrict to promotions where the email channel flag is 'N' or the event channel flag is 'N' (at this scale factor these flags are always 'N', so the clause matches every promotion). Order by item code (the item's business identifier); limit 100 rows.

comment: The reference query embeds a curated 400-element ZIP list inline. The eval supplies that same list as a runtime parameter so the agent doesn't have to retype it; the harness re-injects the official value at scoring time.

zips.description: Comma-separated 5-digit ZIPs to intersect against the preferred-customer-ZIP set.

prompt: For store sales in the 2nd quarter of 1998, report total net profit per store name. Restrict to stores whose ZIP code's first 2 digits match the first 2 digits of any QUALIFYING 5-digit ZIP. A 5-digit ZIP qualifies when its full five-digit value (not just its 2-digit prefix) appears in BOTH of two sets: (a) the curated list provided via the `zips` parameter (a comma-separated string of 5-digit ZIPs â€” declare `parameter zips string;` and split with `split(zips, ',')`), and (b) the set of 5-digit ZIP codes where more than 10 preferred customers (those with the preferred-customer flag set to Y) have their current home address. Order by store name; limit 100 rows.

A single output row with five numeric columns, one per quantity bucket: 1-20, 21-40, 41-60, 61-80, 81-100. Each column's value comes from the store-channel sale lines whose quantity sold falls in that bucket: if the number of sale lines in the bucket exceeds the bucket's threshold (74129, 122840, 56580, 10097, 165306 respectively), the column shows the average extended discount amount across those lines; otherwise it shows the average net paid across those lines. The reason table is not used for any computation or filter -- the result is just a single row, not a grouping.

For customers whose current home address is in one of the counties 'Rush County', 'Toole County', 'Jefferson County', 'Dona Ana County', or 'La Porte County', and who made at least one store sale in the first four calendar months of 2002 (January through April) and either at least one web sale or at least one catalog sale in that same January-through-April 2002 window (matched via the web-sale bill customer or the catalog-sale ship customer), we want to pull a breakdown using the customer's current customer-demographics record, including only customers that have such a current customer-demographics record (a demographic record must be linked to the customer; customers with no linked demographic record — which would otherwise appear as a single all-null demographics group — must be excluded). Group by each combination of gender, marital status, education status, purchase estimate, credit rating, dependent count, dependent-employed count, and dependent-college count. Output exactly these columns in this left-to-right order: gender, marital status, education status, customer count, purchase estimate, customer count, credit rating, customer count, dependent count, customer count, dependent-employed count, customer count, dependent-college count, customer count. The customer count is the same value every time it appears (six identical copies, each placed immediately after education status, purchase estimate, credit rating, dependent count, dependent-employed count, and dependent-college count, respectively). Order the rows by gender, marital status, education status, purchase estimate, credit rating, dependent count, dependent-employed count, then dependent-college count, in that order; limit 100 rows.

Among customers active in both store and web channels in 2001, find those whose web revenue grew proportionally faster than their store revenue from 2001 to 2002. "Revenue" here is the sum of (extended list price minus extended discount amount) over that customer's sales in the given channel in the given year. Include a customer only if they actually have sales in all four buckets - store in 2001, store in 2002, web in 2001, and web in 2002; a customer missing any one of these buckets is excluded entirely (do not treat a missing year as zero). Also require each customer's 2001 total in both channels to be positive. Report billing customer code, first name, last name, and preferred-customer flag (from the 2002 store record); ordered by those four fields with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For web sales of items in the 'Sports', 'Books', or 'Home' categories sold between 1999-02-22 and 1999-03-24, report for each item its business code, description, category, class, and current price, along with the total extended web sales price for that item, and that item's extended sales price as a percentage of the combined extended sales price of all qualifying items in its class (i.e., within the same category and date filters). The percentage is the ratio multiplied by 100 (a value between 0 and 100), not a 0-1 fraction. Report one row per (item code, description, category, class, current price). Order by category, class, item code, description, then the percentage; limit 100 rows.

For physical store sales in the year 2001, return the average ticket quantity, the average extended sales price, the average extended wholesale cost, and the total extended wholesale cost across all qualifying sales. A sale qualifies when it satisfies one demographic-and-price clause AND one address-and-profit clause. The demographic-and-price clause requires that the sale point customer-demographics and the sale point household-demographics jointly match one of three combinations: married with 'Advanced Degree' and per-unit sales price (the unit price, not the line-extended amount) between 100 and 150 and household dependent count of 3; single with 'College' and per-unit sales price between 50 and 100 and household dependent count of 1; or widowed with '2 yr Degree' and per-unit sales price between 150 and 200 and household dependent count of 1. The address-and-profit clause requires that the sale-address record have country 'United States' and match one of: states TX/OH with net profit between 100 and 200; states OR/NM/KY with net profit between 150 and 300; or states VA/TX/MS with net profit between 50 and 250.

Find (brand, class, category) combination tuples for items that appear in all three channels (store sales, catalog sales, and web sales) at some point during 1999 through 2001 inclusive. For any sale matching those tuples in november 2001, aggregate the sum of quantity * list price (don't use precalculated line totals) and count of sale line items (order + item id combos) by the lowercase channel, brand_id, class_id, category_id, returning only groups where the per-channel sales total exceeds the overall average sale value (quantity times list price again, average of non null rows of that calculation only) in 1999-2001 across all channels (make sure this overall average isn't filtered to just november 2001). This average sale filter must be applied *before* rolling up the result by (channel, brand, class, category) producing channel subtotals and a grand total. Report channel (lowercase), numeric brand id, numeric class id, numeric category id, total sales, and total sales line item count, ordered by those four grouping fields with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For catalog sales in the 2nd quarter of year 2001, report the total catalog unit price (use the unit price, not the total sales price) grouped by the ZIP code of the buying customer's own current home address. Include a sale if any one of these holds: the first 5 digits of that ZIP code are one of 85669, 86197, 88274, 83405, 86475, 85392, 85460, 80348, or 81792; or that address's state is CA, WA, or GA; or that sale's per-unit sale price (the same single-unit price used above, not the line/extended total) exceeds 500. Order by ZIP code with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For catalog sales whose ship date falls between 2002-02-01 and 2002-04-02 inclusive, shipping to a directly recorded address in GA (not the customer current address) via a call center located in 'Williamson County', report the count of unique orders, the total extended ship cost, and the total net profit. Two additional constraints - restrict to orders whose line items ship from more than one warehouse (so there exists another line in the same order shipping from a different warehouse) and that have no matching catalog return on the same order number - do NOT include the other conditions when calculation these order level constraints. Order by the order count desc; limit 100 rows.

For store sales sold in the year 2001 with returns as the same customer sk as sold to, where return date falls in the year 2001 or 2002, merged with catalog sales where the store customer sk matches the catalog billing customer sk and the item sks match whose sale date falls in the year 2001 or 2002, report per (item business identifier, item description, store state), separately FOR EACH of three quantity measures - the store-sale ticket quantity, the store-return return quantity, and the catalog-sale ticket quantity - that measure's own count, average, sample standard deviation, and coefficient of variation (stddev divided by average). So there are three separate counts (one per measure), three averages, three standard deviations, and three coefficients. Order by item business identifier, item description, then store state, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For catalog sales in the year 1998, restricted to sales where the pos customer demographics show gender female and education 'Unknown', and where the customer's own birth month is one of January, February, June, August, September, or December, and where the customer's current home address state is one of MS, IN, ND, OK, NM, or VA, and the customer's own current demographic sk is not null; report the average of the following measures: ticket quantity, per-line list price, per-line coupon amount, per-line sales price, per-line net profit, customer birth year, and the dependent count from the sale's pos customer-demographics. All these averages need to be weighted by the line item grain. Roll up these weighted averages over (item code, country, state, county) in exactly that order using the customer's current home address country, state, and county. Order returned rows by country, state, county, item code then by the grouping indicators on country, state, and county (don't show grouping indicators in output, just use for ordering - they are LAST in ordering), all order elements with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For items managed by manager 8, for store sales in November 1998 where the first 5 digits of the customer's own current home address ZIP code differ from those of the selling store's ZIP code, report â€” for each (numeric brand id, brand name, numeric manufacturer id, manufacturer name) combination â€” the total extended store sales price. Use the address on the customer record, not any address attached to the sale itself. Include all four of brand id, brand name, manufacturer id, and manufacturer name in the output. Order by revenue descending, then by brand and manufacturer; limit 100 rows.

For catalog sales of items in the 'Sports', 'Books', or 'Home' categories sold between 1999-02-22 and 1999-03-24, report for each item its business code, description, category, class, and current price, along with the total extended catalog sales price for that item, and that item's extended sales price as a percentage of the total extended sales price of all items in its class. The percentage is the ratio multiplied by 100 (a value between 0 and 100), not a 0-1 fraction. Report one row per (item code, description, category, class, current price). Order by category, class, item code, description, then the percentage, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For each (warehouse name, item code), compare inventory on hand in the 30-day window before 2000-03-11 (from 2000-02-10 up to but not including 2000-03-11) to inventory in the 30-day window starting on and after 2000-03-11 (through 2000-04-10). Sum the on-hand quantity in each window. Restrict to items whose current price is between 0.99 and 1.49. Keep only pairs where the ratio (after divided by before) is between 2/3 and 3/2 (treat the comparison as undefined when the before-sum is zero). Report warehouse name, item code, before-window total, and after-window total, ordered by warehouse name and item code with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

In the year 2000, report the average inventory quantity on hand, grouped with a rollup over (item product name, item brand, item class, item category). Order by the average on-hand ascending with nulls first, then by product name, brand, class, and category all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

Identify 'frequent' items: items where the count of (item unique identifier, first 30 characters of item description, sold date) triples in years 2000 through 2003 exceeds 4 (using the first 30 characters of item description as part of the grouping, not just item id) Filter based on the frequent item sk after identifying. Identify 'best' customers in the store sales channel (store sales meaning the store channel, not catalog or web): for each customer, first compute their all-time store-channel total across every year with no date restriction, as the sum of ticket quantity times per-line sales price; separately, restricted to 2000-2003 only, find the single largest such per-customer total; a customer is 'best' if their all-time total exceeds 50 percent of that 2000-2003 maximum across all customers (not all sales have customers; exclude all null customers from these calculations). All customer filtering should be on the customer sk. Then, for February 2000 sales in catalog and web channels combined, sum quantity times list price per customer (last name, first name) but only for sales whose item is in the frequent set and whose billing customer is in the best-customer set. Report customer last name, first name, and the total sales combining the catalog and web channels into one total per customer, ordered by last name, first name, and sales with nulls first; limit 100 rows; restrict to those with total sales greater than 0

comment: Store-sale-to-return match at market-id-8 stores, customer ZIP = store ZIP, birth country != address country, net paid by customer/color. Gradeable at scale factor >= 1 (the eval default) where item color 'peach' is present; absent at sf=0.01. Same scale artifact as q96.
prompt: For store sales matched to store returns by ticket and item, at stores whose market id is 8, where the customer's current home address ZIP equals the selling store's ZIP and the customer's birth country differs (case-insensitively) from the customer's current address country, sum per-line net paid grouped by (customer last name, first name, store name, address state, store state, item color, item current price, item manager id, item units, item size). Then keep only groups where item color is 'peach', re-aggregate to (customer last name, first name, store name) summing those sub-totals, and keep only re-aggregated groups whose sum exceeds 5 percent of the average sum across all (customer last name, first name, store name, ...) sub-totals from the first stage. Output the last_name, first_name, store name, and total peach sales. Order by customer last name, first name, then store name.

For store sales in April 2001 that were returned by the same customer it was sold to and whose return date is in April through October 2001, matched to catalog sales by customer sk (billed) and item whose sale date is also in April through October 2001, report per (item code, item description, store code, store name) where there is a catalog sale and a store return match; the sum of store-sale net profit, the sum of store-return net loss, and the sum of catalog-sale net profit. Order by item code, item description, store code, then store name; limit 100 rows.

For catalog sales in the year 2000, report the average quantity, average list price, average coupon amount, and average sales price for each item. Filter by the point-of-sale customer-demographic profile recorded directly on each catalog sale: male, single, 'College' education. Do not use the billing customer's current demographic profile. Also restrict to promotions where the email channel flag is 'N' or the event channel flag is 'N' (at this scale factor these flags are always 'N', so the clause matches every promotion). Order by item code (the item's business identifier); limit 100 rows.

For store sales in 2002 at stores in TN, restricted to sales whose recorded point-of-sale customer demographics are single, male, with 'College' education - report the average ticket quantity, average per-line list price, average per-line coupon amount, and average unit price. Produce three groupings combined: per (item code, state) with a group-indicator of 0; per item code (state set to null) with a group-indicator of 1; and an overall total (both item code and state null) with a group-indicator of 1. Order by item code then state with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

Return one row containing 18 values arranged into 6 buckets, computed from store sales. For each bucket compute the average per-line list price, the count of per-line list price values, and the count of unique per-line list price values. Each bucket has a ticket-quantity range and a price-or-coupon-or-cost OR clause. Bucket 1: ticket quantity 0 to 5, and (list price between 8 and 18 OR coupon amount between 459 and 1459 OR wholesale cost between 57 and 77). Bucket 2: ticket quantity 6 to 10, and (list price 90 to 100 OR coupon 2323 to 3323 OR wholesale 31 to 51). Bucket 3: ticket quantity 11 to 15, and (list price 142 to 152 OR coupon 12214 to 13214 OR wholesale 79 to 99). Bucket 4: ticket quantity 16 to 20, and (list price 135 to 145 OR coupon 6071 to 7071 OR wholesale 38 to 58). Bucket 5: ticket quantity 21 to 25, and (list price 122 to 132 OR coupon 836 to 1836 OR wholesale 17 to 37). Bucket 6: ticket quantity 26 to 30, and (list price 154 to 164 OR coupon 7326 to 8326 OR wholesale 7 to 27).

For store sales in September 1999, matched to store returns from the same customer sk, item sk, and ticket whose return date falls between September and December 1999, matched to catalog sales by the same customer (billed) and item whose sale year is 1999, 2000, or 2001, report per (item code, item description, store code, store name) - non sk Ids for item/store - the total store-sale ticket quantity, the total store-return return quantity, and the total catalog-sale ticket quantity. Order by item code, item description, store code, then store name; limit 100 rows.

For target returning customers (the customers who made the web returns) whose total web-return amount in 2002 for a given return-location state exceeded 1.2 times the average total web-return amount across all customers who who returned items in the same state in that year; return only those whose own current home address state is GA (ratios calculated before this restriction). Report each qualifying customer's customer code (the stable business identifier), salutation, first name, last name, preferred-customer flag, birth day, birth month, birth year, birth country, login, email address, last-review-date surrogate, and total web return amount. Order by those thirteen fields in the order listed, all with nulls first; filter out any return locations that are null. limit 100 rows.

For each county and each calendar quarter in the year 2000, compute total extended store sales price (grouped by the county of the address recorded on the store sale — the customer's sale address, not the store's own location county) and total extended web sales price (using the web-sale billing-address county). Return the county and sales year alongside four ratios: web Q2-over-Q1, store Q2-over-Q1, web Q3-over-Q2, and store Q3-over-Q2 (computed as floating-point divisions). Keep only counties whose web Q2-over-Q1 ratio exceeds their store Q2-over-Q1 ratio AND whose web Q3-over-Q2 ratio exceeds their store Q3-over-Q2 ratio (each ratio is undefined when its denominator is zero). Order by county asc, nulls first.

For catalog sales of items from manufacturer 48 sold between 2000-01-27 and 2000-04-26 inclusive, return the total extended discount amount across only those sales whose per-line extended discount amount exceeds 1.3 times the average extended discount amount for that same item across all of its catalog sales in the same date window. Limit to 100 rows (the result is a single value).

For sales across all channels in May 1998 (year 1998, month 5) to sales billing addresses with GMT offset -5, for items whose numeric manufacturer id appears among the manufacturers of items in the 'Electronics' category, sum the extended sales price per numeric manufacturer id. Report numeric manufacturer id and the combined total, ordered by the combined total ascending, then numeric manufacturer id, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

List the last name, first name, salutation, preferred-customer flag, and store-sale ticket number for store-sale tickets, along with the count of line items on that ticket. Restrict to tickets sold in years 1999, 2000, or 2001, on a day of the month that is between 1 and 3 or between 25 and 28, at a store in 'Williamson County', for sales whose household demographics recorded on the sale have a buy potential of '>10000' or 'Unknown', a vehicle count greater than 0, and a dependent count divided by vehicle count (computed as a decimal) greater than 1.2. Among the sales meeting all of those restrictions, count the line items for each (ticket, customer) combination, then keep only tickets whose resulting total line-item count is between 15 and 20 (this threshold is applied to the aggregated per-ticket count, after the restrictions above). Report all qualifying rows; do not cap or limit the result set. Include the customer's name fields. Order by last name, first name, salutation, then preferred-customer flag descending, then ticket number, all with nulls first.

List customers along with demographic and address attributes for customers who had a store sale in one of the first three quarters of year 2002, AND who also had at least one web sale (by billing customer) OR catalog sale (by ship customer) in the same first-three-quarters-of-2002 window. Use the customer's own current home address state and current customer-demographic record (gender, marital status, dependent count, employed-dependent count, college-dependent count); include only customers who have such a current customer-demographic record (exclude any customer with no linked demographic-profile record — do not emit a catch-all null-demographics group). For each combination of state, gender, marital status, dependent count, employed-dependent count, and college-dependent count, return a row with the state/gender/marital_status, then in the order of dependent count, employed dependent count, and college dependent count, a group of 1. the field; 2. the count of the customer sk (yes, same for all), 3. the min of the field, 4 the max of the field, 5 the avg of the field. (so 3 groupings of five columns. Order results by state, gender, marital status, dependent count, employed-dependent count, then college-dependent count, all sorts with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in year 2001 at stores in TN, compute the gross-margin ratio (sum of net profit divided by sum of extended sales price) at three levels of an item-category-and-class hierarchy: by category and class, by category alone (rolling up class), and grand total (rolling up both). Mark each row with its hierarchy level (0 for the leaf level, 1 for category subtotal, 2 for the grand total). Within each hierarchy level, rank rows by gross margin ascending, partitioned by the parent: leaf rows are ranked within their category, the subtotal rows are ranked among themselves, and the grand total is its own rank. Report gross margin, category, class, hierarchy level, and the within-parent rank. Order by hierarchy level descending with nulls first, then by category for the leaf rows with nulls first, then by within-parent rank with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

List the item code, description, and current price of items whose current price is between 68 and 98, that are from manufacturers 677, 940, 694, or 808, that had between 100 and 500 units of inventory on hand on dates between 2000-02-01 and 2000-04-01, and where the item sk appears in catalog sales anywhere. Order by item code; limit 100 rows.

Count the number of unique (customer last name, customer first name, sale date) combinations that appear in all three of store sales, catalog sales (by billing customer), and web sales (by billing customer) in the year 2000. Null customer IDs must be filtered from all; preserve null first/last names, etc. Return a single count value. Limit 100 rows.

For inventory measurements during year 2001, compute for each (warehouse sk item sk, month-of-year) combination the mean and the sample standard deviation of the on-hand quantity, then derive the coefficient of variation (standard deviation divided by mean, treated as null when mean is 0). Keep only combinations whose coefficient of variation exceeds 1. From those qualifying combinations, pair each one in month 1 (January) with the same warehouse and item in month 2 (February), keeping only pairs that occur in both months; report ten columns total: for January the warehouse surrogate id, item, month-of-year, mean, and coefficient of variation, then the same five columns for February. Report the mean and the coefficient of variation only — do NOT include the standard deviation itself. Order by warehouse, item, January's month-of-year, January's mean, January's coefficient of variation, then February's month-of-year, February's mean, and February's coefficient of variation, all with nulls first. Return all qualifying rows; do not apply a row limit.

For catalog sales of items whose current price is between 0.99 and 1.49, sold on dates between 2000-02-10 and 2000-04-10, and only for sales where the warehouse is recorded, report two figures grouped by warehouse state and item code (the item's stable business identifier, not the surrogate key): the total of (the unit price (not the total price) minus any refunded cash from a matching catalog return for the same order and item) for sales before 2000-03-11, and the same unit-price-based total for sales on or after 2000-03-11. Match catalog returns to catalog sales by order number and item; treat a missing refunded cash amount as 0, and report 0 (not null) for a period with no qualifying sales. Order by warehouse state then item code; limit 100 rows.

List the unique product names of items whose numeric manufacturer id is between 1 and 500 inclusive, restricted to items whose manufacturer (the manufacturer-text field, not the numeric manufacturer id) is shared by at least one other item matching any one of eight detailed attribute profiles. The eight profiles are: Books / tan / Oz / N/A; Electronics / purple / Ton / N/A; Men / misty / Box / medium; Books / medium / Tsp / N/A; Books / midnight / Gram / N/A; Books / pale / Pound / N/A; Electronics / khaki / Pallet / N/A; Electronics / mint / Gross / N/A. Each profile constrains category, color, units, and size. Order by product name; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in the 11th month of year 2000, report total extended sales price by item category, restricted to items managed by manager 1. Order by total descending, then year, numeric category id, and category; limit 100 rows.

For each store, report a sales-price total split into seven separate day-of-the-week columns (Sunday through Saturday), for sales in the year 2000, restricted to stores with a GMT offset of -5. Sum the unit price, not the total price. Order by store name and store code (the store's stable business identifier); limit 100 rows.

For store sales at the store whose surrogate id is 1 (the internal row key, not a business code), rank items by their average net profit. Keep only items whose average net profit exceeds 90 percent of the average net profit on sales at that same store where the sale's address key is missing (null). Take the top 10 items by ascending rank (best performers - lowest average net profit first) and the top 10 items by descending rank (worst performers - highest average net profit first), then pair them position by position by rank. For each pair, report the rank, the best-performer product name, and the worst-performer product name. Order by rank ascending, best-performer product name descending, then worst-performer product name descending; limit 100 rows.

For web sales in the 2nd quarter (use dimension value, do not calculate from the date - quarters do not align) of year 2001, report the total web unit price (the sum of the unit price, not the total price) grouped by the billing customer's current home address ZIP code and city. Include a sale only if either the first 5 digits of that ZIP code are one of 85669, 86197, 88274, 83405, 86475, 85392, 85460, 80348, or 81792, OR the item belongs to the same item-id group as any item whose internal item key is one of 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, or 29. Use the address on the customer's current address record. Order by ZIP code then city; limit 100 rows.

List the customer's last name and first name, the city of the customer's current home address, the city of the address recorded on the store-sale ticket itself, the store-sale ticket number, and per-ticket totals of coupon amount and net profit. Restrict to store sales made on a Saturday or Sunday in years 1999, 2000, or 2001, at stores in the cities 'Fairview' or 'Midway', to households recorded on the sale whose dependent count is 4 or whose vehicle count is 3. Total coupon amount and net profit per ticket, customer, sale address, and sale-address city. Include the customer's name fields and current home address; keep only rows where the customer's current home address city differs from the city of the sale address. Order by last name, first name, customer's current address city, the sale-address city, then ticket number, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For each (item category, item brand, store name, store company name) combination, compute monthly totals of the store unit price (not the total price) across months of year 1999 plus December 1998 and January 2000. Within each (category, brand, store, company, year) group, compute the average of those monthly totals (the average monthly sales). Within each (category, brand, store, company) group, order the months chronologically by year then month-of-year and assign each a sequence number. Keep only rows for year 1999 where the average monthly sales is positive and the relative deviation between the month's total and the average monthly sales (absolute difference divided by average monthly sales) exceeds 0.1. For each such row, also report the previous month's total (lag, from the immediately prior month in the same category-brand-store-company sequence) and the next month's total (lead). Report category, brand, store name, store company name, year, month-of-year, average monthly sales, this month's total, the prior month's total, and the next month's total. Order by the difference between this month's total and the average monthly sales (signed), then by each of those reported columns in turn; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in year 2000, report the total store-sale quantity. Use the customer-demographic record linked directly to the sale (not the customer's current one). Restrict to sales matching BOTH of two independent conditions. Condition 1 (demographic + unit price, where 'unit price' means the per-unit sales price actually charged, not the list price) — any one of: (a) married with '4 yr Degree' and unit price between 100 and 150; (b) divorced with '2 yr Degree' and price between 50 and 100; (c) single with 'College' and price between 150 and 200. Condition 2 (billing address + net profit) — any one of: (a) United States in CO, OH, or TX with net profit between 0 and 2000; (b) US in OR, MN, or KY with net profit between 150 and 3000; (c) US in VA, CA, or MS with net profit between 50 and 25000. The demographic and address conditions are evaluated as two separate OR-groups joined by AND (any demographic option may pair with any address option), not as three fixed bundles. Return a single total quantity value.

For December 2001, identify products with unusually favorable return behavior that nonetheless each had at least one high-value return. Only consider sales transactions whose profit was more than one dollar, with positive revenue (net amount paid) and positive unit sales, AND that are tied to a return exceeding $10,000. A product appears only if it has at least one such transaction, and EVERY measure below is computed over these qualifying transactions only (not over all of the product's sales). Within each sales channel (web, catalog, and store), evaluate two measures per product: the proportion of returned units relative to units sold (sum of returned units divided by sum of units sold), and the proportion of returned value relative to revenue (sum of returned amount divided by sum of net amount paid). Within each channel, rank products on each measure with lower values ranking better (best = rank 1). Include any product that ranks among the ten best in its channel on either measure. Return exactly these five columns and no others: channel - the sales channel in lowercase, item - the item's sk field; return_ratio - returned units divided by sold units; return_rank - the product's rank within its channel by return_ratio; currency_rank - the product's rank within its channel by returned value relative to revenue. Sort by channel, then return_rank, then currency_rank, then item - all ascending with nulls first. Return no more than 100 rows.

For each store, count the number of store-sale-line-item-to-store-return pairings (matched by ticket number, item, and customer) where the return occurred in August 2001, the same customer did the return as the sale, and the sale store sk is recorded, bucketed by the elapsed days between the sale date and the return date into five columns: 30 days or fewer, 31 to 60 days, 61 to 90 days, 91 to 120 days, and more than 120 days. For each combination of store name, numeric company id, street number, street name, street type, suite number, city, county, state, and ZIP, report the five bucketed counts. Order by those same store attributes in the same order; limit 100 rows.

In the year 2000, compute per-item (identifying each item by its internal surrogate item key, not the business item code), per-day running totals of the unit price (not the total price) for both web sales and store sales (each running total is cumulative across days, partitioned by item, within the year). Combine the two streams by item and date so each (item, date) combination appears once with whichever side(s) have data, keeping rows that exist on either side. For each (item, date) row, also compute the running maximum of the web running total and the running maximum of the store running total (cumulative across dates, partitioned by item, taking the running totals from the combined row). Keep only rows where the running maximum of the web side exceeds the running maximum of the store side. Report the internal surrogate item key, date, web running total, store running total, web running maximum, and store running maximum. Order by item then date, with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in the 11th month of year 2000, report total extended sales price by brand, restricted to items managed by manager 1. Report the year, numeric brand id, and brand alongside the total. Order by year, then total descending, then numeric brand id; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in the year 2000, restricted to items in one of two profiles - either category in 'Books', 'Children', 'Electronics' with class in 'personal', 'portable', 'reference', 'self-help' and brand in 'scholaramalgamalg #14', 'scholaramalgamalg #7', 'exportiunivamalg #9', or 'scholaramalgamalg #9'; or category in 'Women', 'Music', 'Men' with class in 'accessories', 'classical', 'fragrances', 'pants' and brand in 'amalgimporto #1', 'edu packscholar #1', 'exportiimporto #1', or 'importoamalg #1' - compute the total of the unit price (not the total price), for store sales with a recorded store, for each (numeric manufacturer id, quarter-of-year) combination. Then for each numeric manufacturer id compute the average of those per-quarter totals (the average quarterly sales). Keep only (manufacturer, quarter) rows where the average quarterly sales is positive and the relative deviation between the row's total and the average (absolute difference divided by average) exceeds 0.1. Report numeric manufacturer id, the per-quarter total, and the average quarterly sales. Order by average quarterly sales, then the per-quarter total, then numeric manufacturer id; limit 100 rows. Output exactly these three columns and no others — in particular do not include the quarter-of-year column.

Identify customers who bought any item in the 'Women' category and 'maternity' class via catalog (by billing customer) or web (by billing customer) in December 1998. For each such customer, sum the extended store-sale price from store sales they made during a 3-month window starting the month after December 1998 (i.e., months whose month-sequence is between the month-sequence-of-Dec-1998 plus 1 and the month-sequence-of-Dec-1998 plus 3, inclusive), restricted to store sales at stores whose county and state both match the customer's current home address's county and state. Include only customers who actually had at least one such qualifying store sale in that window; a customer with no qualifying store sale is dropped entirely, not counted as a zero-total segment. Bucket each customer's total into a segment by dividing the total by 50 and rounding to the nearest integer. For each segment report the segment, the count of customers in that segment, and segment times 50. Order by segment, then customer count, then segment-times-50, with nulls first on the first two; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in the 11th month of year 1999, report total extended sales price by brand, restricted to items managed by manager 28. Order by total descending, then numeric brand id; limit 100 rows.

For sales across all channels in February 2001 to sales billing addresses with GMT offset -5, for items whos item codes have any item variant with the color 'slate', 'blanched', or 'burnished', sum the extended sales price. Report item code and the combined total. Only item codes that actually have a matching sale in this period and region appear; do not pad the result with item codes that had no such sale, and do not add any minimum-total filter. Order by total then item code, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For each (item category, item brand, call center name) combination, compute monthly catalog-sales per-unit price totals (sum the per-unit sales price, not the extended line-total price) across months of year 1999 (you'll need 1998/2000 boundary months to calculate the edge ratios). Within each (category, brand, call center, year) group, compute the average of those monthly totals (the average monthly sales). Within each (category, brand, call center) group, order the months chronologically by year then month-of-year and assign each a sequence number. Final output should show only the 1999 rows where the average monthly sales is positive and the relative deviation between the month's total and the average monthly sales (absolute difference divided by average) exceeds 0.1. For each such row, also report the previous month's total (lag) and the next month's total (lead) from the same (category, brand, call center) sequence. Report category, brand, call center, year, month-of-year, average monthly sales, this month's total, the prior month's total, and the next month's total. Order by the signed difference between this month's total and the average monthly sales with nulls first, then by each of the reported columns; limit 100 rows.

Find items whose extended sales price totals are nearly equal across all three channels (store, catalog, and web) within the calendar week that contains 2000-01-03. For each channel, sum extended sales price per item code, restricted to dates falling in the same week as 2000-01-03. Restrict to item codes that appear in all three channels. Keep only items where each pair of channel totals is within 10 percent of each other in both directions (so for any two channels A and B, A is between 0.9*B and 1.1*B). Report the item code (the item's stable business identifier, not the surrogate key), the store total, the store total as a percentage of the three-channel average (store total divided by the average, multiplied by 100), the catalog total and its percentage of the average, the web total and its percentage of the average, and the three-channel average itself. Order by item code then the store total, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For each (store, calendar week) combination, sum the store unit price (not the total price) split into seven day-of-week columns (Sunday through Saturday); leave a day's total null when the store has no sales on that weekday in the week (do not substitute zero). Then pair weeks across years: take rows whose week falls in the year 2001 (the 'this year' set) and rows whose week falls in the next the year 2002 (the 'next year' set), matched by same store and where the 'this year' week sequence equals the 'next year' week sequence minus 52. Keep only pairs where there is one week_seq in 2001 and one in 2002. For each matching pair report the store name, store code (the store's stable business identifier), and 'this year' week sequence, plus the ratio of this-year-Sunday to next-year-Sunday, this-year-Monday to next-year-Monday, and so on through Saturday; report each ratio directly and leave it null when either side is null or the denominator is zero (do not substitute zero). Order by store name, store code, then this-year week sequence, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For sales across all channels in September 1998 to billing addresses with a GMT offset of -5, for items whose item code matches the item code of any item in the 'Music' category, sum the extended sales price. Report item code and the combined total. Order by item code then total, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in November 1998 of items in the 'Jewelry' category, restricted to stores with GMT offset -5 and customers whose current home address has GMT offset -5, compute two totals: the total extended sales price across all such sales, and the subset of that total where the sale's promotion has any of its direct-mail, email, or TV channel flags equal to 'Y'. Report the promotion-channel total, the overall total, and the promotion total as a percentage of the overall total (multiplied by 100). Order by promotion total then overall total; limit 100 rows.

For web sales whose ship date falls in the year 2000, and only for sales where the warehouse, shipping mode, and web site are all recorded (i.e. each is linked — its identifier is present; a linked warehouse/ship-mode/web-site whose name happens to be blank still counts as recorded, so do not exclude a row merely because the name is null), report the count of web-sale line items (each a unique order + item combination) bucketed by shipping lag (ship date minus sold date, in days) into five columns: 30 days or fewer, 31 to 60 days, 61 to 90 days, 91 to 120 days, and more than 120 days. Report 0 (not null) for any bucket with no matching line items. For each combination of the first 20 characters of the warehouse name, the shipping-mode type, and the web site name, report the five bucketed counts. Order by those three attributes, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in the year 2000, restricted to items in one of two profiles - either category in 'Books', 'Children', 'Electronics' with class in 'personal', 'portable', 'reference', 'self-help' and brand in 'scholaramalgamalg #14', 'scholaramalgamalg #7', 'exportiunivamalg #9', or 'scholaramalgamalg #9'; or category in 'Women', 'Music', 'Men' with class in 'accessories', 'classical', 'fragrances', 'pants' and brand in 'amalgimporto #1', 'edu packscholar #1', 'exportiimporto #1', or 'importoamalg #1' - compute the total of the unit price (not the total price), for non_null store sks, for each (numeric manager id, month-of-year) combination. Then for each numeric manager id compute the average of those per-month totals (the average monthly sales). Keep only (manager, month) rows where the average monthly sales is positive and the relative deviation between the row's total and the average (absolute difference divided by average) exceeds 0.1. Report exactly these three columns and no others (in particular do not include the month-of-year): numeric manager id, the per-month total, and the average monthly sales. Order by numeric manager id, then average monthly sales, then the per-month total; limit 100 rows.

Identify items sold in store sales where the cumulative extended list price (across catalog sales for that item) exceeds twice the cumulative refund amount (sum of refunded cash plus reversed charge plus store credit on catalog returns matched by item and catalog order number). For each such item involved in a store sale that also has a matching store return (on ticket number and item), aggregate by (item product name, item, store name, store ZIP, sale-address fields, customer's current-address fields, sale year, customer's first-sales date year, customer's first-ship-to date year). Restrict to items whose color is in 'purple', 'burlywood', 'indian', 'spring', 'floral', or 'medium' and whose current price is between 65 and 74 inclusive (the intersection of two stated ranges). Restrict further to sales where the customer-demographics on the sale and the customer's current customer-demographics have different marital statuses. For each group, count the number of sale lines and sum the per-unit wholesale cost, the per-unit list price, and the coupon amount (the unit values, not the line-extended totals). Then self-pair these aggregated rows on item and matching store name and store ZIP, where the first row has sale year 1999 and the second has sale year 2000 and the second row's count is no greater than the first row's count. Include an item (with its matching store name and ZIP) only if it has a qualifying aggregated row in BOTH 1999 and 2000 — drop items that appear in only one of the two years, and do not treat a missing year as a zero count. Report product name, store name, store ZIP, both addresses' street numbers, street names, cities, and ZIPs, the first row's year and count and three sums, then the second row's three sums, year, and count. Order by product name, store name, then the second row's count, then the first row's wholesale-cost sum, then the second row's wholesale-cost sum.

For store sales in the year 1998 that were made at a recorded store (exclude any sale with no associated store), compute revenue (the sum of the unit price, not the total price) per (store, item). For each store, compute the average across items of that per-item revenue. After computing the 1998 per-item revenue and, per store, the average of those per-item revenues, keep only the (store, item) rows whose per-item revenue is no more than 10 percent of that store average (this comparison is applied to the aggregated revenues, after the year-1998 restriction). Report store name, item description, the per-item revenue, item current price, item wholesale cost, and item brand. Order by store name, item description, store, then item, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For web and catalog sales in 2001 made during the time-of-day window between second 30838 and 59638, shipped by a carrier named 'DHL' or 'BARIAN', and with a recorded warehouse, grouped by warehouse name, warehouse square footage, warehouse city, county, state, country, a ship-carriers label with the constant value 'DHL,BARIAN', and year: report, for each of the twelve months, the month's total sales, the month's sales per square foot (that month's total sales divided by the warehouse square footage), and the month's total net - together with the eight grouping fields. (so 34 total columns; 24 metrics + the grouping fields). A sale's monthly sales amount is its quantity times the extended sales price for web sales and its quantity times the per-unit sales price for catalog sales; a sale's monthly net amount is its quantity times net paid for web sales and its quantity times net-paid-including-tax for catalog sales. Report 0 for any month with no qualifying sales. Order by warehouse name then year, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For store sales in 2000, considering only sales attributed to a known store, calculate summed sales as the sum of per-unit sales price multiplied by quantity, with a null product being coalesced to zero. Compute this measure using a standard ROLLUP in exactly this order: item category, item class, item brand, item product name, year, quarter-of-year, month-of-year, and store code, where store code is the store's stable business identifier. Calculate a rank across all grouping keys ordering by summed sales descending. All rows whose resulting category is null—including the grand-total row and any other rows with a null category—must belong to the same rank partition; do not create a separate partition for the grand total. Keep rows whose rank is 100 or less. Report category, class, brand, product name, year, quarter-of-year, month-of-year, store code, summed sales, and rank. Order by those ten reported columns in that order, all ascending with nulls first. Limit 100 rows.

For store-sale tickets sold in 1999, 2000, or 2001, on a day of the month between 1 and 2, at a store in the city of Fairview or Midway, where the sale's recorded household demographics have a dependent count of 4 or a vehicle count of 3, and where the customer's current home address city differs from the city recorded on the sale address, report per-ticket totals of extended sales price, extended list price, and extended tax. Output the customer's last name and first name, the customer's current home city, the city recorded on the sale address, the ticket number, and the three per-ticket totals. Order by last name then ticket number, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For customers whose current home address state is KY, GA, or NM, who made at least one store sale between months 4 and 6 of year 2001, and who made no web sale (as the billing customer) and no catalog sale (as the ship customer) during those same three months (April through June 2001), report the count of qualifying customers grouped by their current customer-demographic profile's gender, marital status, education status, purchase estimate, and credit rating. Output those five demographic attributes alongside three identical count columns. Order by gender, marital status, education status, purchase estimate, then credit rating; limit 100 rows.

For store sales whose sold date falls in the year 2000, considering only sales attributable to a known store, and only stores in the top 5 states by total store net profit over the same period, report total net profit rolled up by store state and store county. Alongside the total, include a hierarchy level (0 for state-and-county rows, 1 for state-only subtotals, 2 for the grand total) and a rank within each parent level: state rows are ranked by net profit descending within their hierarchy level, county rows are ranked by net profit descending within their state. Order by hierarchy level descending, then by state for the detail rows, then by the within-parent rank; limit 100 rows.

For items managed by manager 1, combine extended sales price across web, catalog, and store sales sold in the 11th month of year 1999, restricted to sale times whose meal-time classification is either 'breakfast' or 'dinner'. Report total extended sales price grouped by numeric item brand id, brand, and the hour and minute of the sale time. Order by total descending, then numeric brand id, hour, and minute, all with nulls first. Report all rows; do not cap or limit the result set.

For catalog sales in 1999 where the billing time recorded (not current) household demographic's buy potential is '>10000', the billing time recorded customer demographic (not current) marital status is 'D', and the sale took more than 5 days to ship, find all warehouse inventory for the same item in the same week sequences where the inventory quantity on hand was less than the catalog sales quantity. Match sales to inventory by item sk and weel sequece only, regardless of which warehouse held the stock. The warehouse match must not be null and the items must be the same.  Return the item description, the name of the warehouse that held the inventory, the week sequence the item was sold in, and three counts of order ids: those with no promotion, those with a promotion, and the total. Order the results by total orders descending (nulls first), then by item description, warehouse name, and week sequence (each nulls first). Limit to 100 rows.

List the customer's last name, first name, salutation, preferred-customer flag, store-sale ticket number, and the count of line items on that ticket. Restrict to tickets sold in years 1999, 2000, or 2001, on a day of the month between 1 and 2, at a store in Orange County, Bronx County, Franklin Parish, or Williamson County, to the household recorded on the sale whose buy potential is 'Unknown' or '>10000', whose vehicle count is greater than 0, and whose dependent-count divided by vehicle-count is greater than 1. Consider only sales with an identified customer. Among the sales meeting all of those restrictions, count the line items for each (ticket, customer) combination, then keep only tickets whose resulting total line-item count is between 1 and 5 (this threshold is applied to the aggregated per-ticket count, after the restrictions above). Order by line-item count descending, then last name ascending, then ticket number ascending, then customer.

For each customer, compute total net paid from store sales and total net paid from web sales for each of years 2001 and 2002. Keep only customers who have both store sales and web sales in each of 2001 and 2002 (a customer missing any of those four channel-year totals does not qualify), whose 2001 store total and 2001 web total are both strictly positive, and for whom the ratio of 2002 web total to 2001 web total is greater than the ratio of 2002 store total to 2001 store total. Output the customer code (the stable business identifier), first name, and last name for the qualifying customers. Order by customer code with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For items in the 'Books' category, compute net sales quantity (sold quantity minus any matched return quantity, with missing returns treated as zero, but null sold quantity is still null) and net sales amount (extended sales price minus any matched return amount, missing returns treated as zero, missing sales null) across store, catalog, and web channels, matching returns to sales by order and item. Combine all three channels' then aggregate to year, numeric brand id, numeric class id, numeric category id, and numeric manufacturer id. Then compare each (brand, class, category, manufacturer) combination between year 2002 and year 2001, considering only combinations that have actual sales in BOTH years - a combination missing from either year is excluded, never treated as zero - and keep only those where the 2002 quantity divided by the 2001 quantity is less than 0.9. Output the previous and current years, the brand/class/category/manufacturer numeric ids, the previous and current quantities, and the differences (current-year minus previous-year) in quantity and in amount. Order by quantity difference, then amount difference; limit 100 rows.

Across all channels find lines with a specific per channel missing-reference situation: store sales whose selling store reference is null, web sales whose shipping customer reference is null, and catalog sales whose shipping address reference is null. Consider only lines whose sale date is recorded. For each such line, capture the year, quarter of year, and item category. Report grouped by channel label ('store', 'web', 'catalog'), a label naming which reference is missing (use the raw string literals 'ss_store_sk', 'ws_ship_customer_sk', and 'cs_ship_addr_sk' verbatim as the labels: these are fixed output text, not columns to look up), year, quarter of year, and item category the count of lines and the total extended sales price. Order by channel, the missing-reference label, year, quarter, then category, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For the period 2000-08-23 through 2000-09-22, report sales, returns, and profit by channel and outlet. Build sales and return aggregates independently: filter sales by sold date and returns by return date, so a return qualifies even when its original sale occurred outside the period. For the store channel, group sales by selling-store SK and returns by return-store SK, then match those aggregates on SK. For the catalog channel, group sales by sale call-center SK and returns by return call-center SK, then match those aggregates on SK. For the web channel, group sales by selling web-page SK and returns by return web-page SK, then match those aggregates on SK. In every channel, require the sales-side outlet SK to be recorded, keep only outlets with qualifying sales, and exclude return-only outlets. Sales is the sum of extended sales price. Returns is the matched return-amount total, with a missing matched total treated as zero. Profit is the sales-side net-profit total minus the matched return-side net-loss total, with missing matched loss treated as zero. Label the channels 'store channel', 'catalog channel', and 'web channel'. Union the channel results and roll up by channel and outlet to produce per-outlet rows, per-channel subtotals, and a grand total. Leave channel and outlet null on rollup levels. Sum sales, returns, and profit at every rollup level. Order by channel and outlet ascending with nulls first, then by returns descending; limit 100 rows.

For sales in year 2000 with a recorded customer SK, group never-returned store-sale lines by year, item SK, and customer SK. Treat a sale line as never returned only when no return record matches its ticket number and item SK. For each group, separately sum the sold quantity, recorded per-unit wholesale-cost value, and recorded per-unit sales-price value; do not use extended amounts and do not divide either price total by quantity. Compute the same three sums for never-returned web and catalog sale lines, grouping by year, item SK, and billing-customer SK and matching returns by order number and item SK. Match the web and catalog groups to the store groups on year, item SK, and customer SK, and retain only store groups whose combined web-plus-catalog quantity is greater than zero. Report the year, item SK, customer SK, store quantity divided by combined web-plus-catalog quantity rounded to two decimal places, the three store-channel sums, and the three combined web-plus-catalog sums. Use the integer SK values attached directly to the sale records; do not translate them to stable business IDs or item codes. Treat a missing web or catalog sum as zero. Order by year, item SK, and customer SK ascending with nulls first; then by store quantity, store wholesale-cost sum, and store sales-price sum descending with nulls last; then by combined other-channel quantity, wholesale-cost sum, and sales-price sum ascending with nulls first; then by the rounded ratio ascending with nulls first. Limit 100 rows.

Find the total coupon amount and net profit by customer sk, store city, sale address. Restrict to store sales made on a Monday from 1999 to 2001, at stores with between 200 and 295 employees, to households recorded on the sale whose dependent count is 6 or whose vehicle count is greater than 2. Consider only sales with an identified customer. Ensuring that unique customeres with the same address and name are NOT merged, only show in results the customer last name, first name first 30 characters of the store city, the ticket number, and the two aggregates. Order by last name, first name, the 30-character store city, net profit, then ticket number - all ordering must be nulls first except for ticket_number ; limit 100 rows.

For the period 2000-08-23 through 2000-09-22, restricted to items whose current price is greater than 50 and to promotions whose TV channel flag is explicitly 'N', report sales, returns, and profit rolled up by channel and outlet. For the store channel the outlet identifier is the store's business code prefixed with 'store'; for the catalog channel it is the catalog page's business code prefixed with 'catalog_page'; for the web channel it is the web site's business code prefixed with 'web_site'. In each channel, profit is the channel's net profit minus matched return net loss (returns matched by order/ticket and item), and returns is the channel's return amount with missing treated as zero. If net profit is null, the entire net_profit minus return amount result should be null. Sum sales, returns, and profit at each subtotal level so we get per-outlet rows, per-channel subtotals, and a grand total. Label the channel as 'store channel', 'catalog channel', or 'web channel'. Include only sales whose outlet (store, catalog page, or web site) is recorded. Order by channel and outlet identifier, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For catalog returns in year 2000, considering only returns whose returning-address state is recorded, compute each (returning customer, returning-address state) combination's total return amount including tax. Then keep only customers whose total exceeds 1.2 times the average total within their returning-address state, and whose own current home address state is GA. For each such customer, output customer code (the stable business identifier), salutation, first name, last name, the current address's street number, street name, street type, suite number, city, county, state, ZIP, country, GMT offset, and location type, alongside the total return amount including tax. Order by all sixteen output columns in their listed order; limit 100 rows.

List the item code, description, and current price of items whose current price is between 62 and 92, that are from manufacturers 129, 270, 821, or 423, that had at least one inventory snapshot recording between 100 and 500 units on hand (at any single warehouse on any snapshot date) during 2000-05-25 to 2000-07-24, and where the specific item record (by item_sk) appears in store sales data. Order by item code; limit 100 rows.

For each item code (treating every surrogate version of the same item code as one item), compute three return quantities - total store-return quantity, total catalog-return quantity, and total web-return quantity - restricted to returns whose returned date falls in any of the calendar weeks that contain 2000-06-30, 2000-09-27, or 2000-11-17. Keep only items that have at least one return record (a return row, regardless of quantity) in each of the three channels. Each channel's total is the sum of its recorded return quantities; individual return rows with no recorded quantity simply do not contribute. A channel's total counts as unknown only when none of its return rows recorded any quantity at all, in which case that channel's percentage and the item's three-channel average are likewise unknown. For each such item, report the item code, the store-return quantity, the store-return quantity as a percentage of the three-channel total averaged across channels (the formula is store qty divided by the three-channel sum, divided by 3, times 100), the catalog-return quantity with its analogous percentage, the web-return quantity with its analogous percentage, and the three-channel average (sum divided by 3). Order by item code then store-return quantity, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

List the customer code (the stable business identifier) and full name (formatted as 'last name, first name', using empty strings for missing parts) for each all demographic matches between any customer with a current home address in the city of Edgewood and any store return on the customers customer-demographic profile type equals the (point in time) demographic profile from the return (not just the customer associated with the return; any demographic matching customer, drop null demographic keys); the income band of the customer's current household has lower bound at least 38128 and upper bound at most 88128. Report one row per non-null qualifying (customer, ticket_number, item_id) combo, but only return the customer code and full name. Order by customer code with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For web sales sold in year 2000 that were returned, for each return reason description report the first 20 characters of that description, the average sold quantity, the average refunded cash on the matching return, and the average return fee. Match the web return to the web sale by item and order number. Restrict to records satisfying BOTH of these independent conditions. Condition 1 (demographics + sales price) — one of: (a) both the refunded and returning customer-demographic profiles are married with 'Advanced Degree' and the sales price is between 100 and 150; (b) both single with 'College' and sales price between 50 and 100; (c) both widowed with '2 yr Degree' and sales price between 150 and 200. Condition 2 (refunded address + net profit) — one of: (a) United States in IN, OH, or NJ with net profit between 100 and 200; (b) US in WI, CT, or KY with net profit between 150 and 300; (c) US in LA, IA, or AR with net profit between 50 and 250. The returning and refunded demographics must agree on marital status and education. Order by the 20-character reason description, then the three averages; limit 100 rows.

For web sales whose sold date falls in the year 2000, report total net paid rolled up by item category and item class. Alongside the total, include a hierarchy level (0 for category-and-class rows, 1 for category-only subtotals, 2 for the grand total) and a rank within each parent level: category rows are ranked by net paid descending within their hierarchy level, class rows are ranked by net paid descending within their category. Order by hierarchy level descending (nulls first), then by category for the detail rows (nulls first), then by the within-parent rank (nulls first); limit 100 rows.

Count the unique (last name, first name, sale date) combinations from store sales in the year 2000 whose (last name, first name, sale date) does not appear among catalog sales in 2000 and does not appear among web sales in 2000. Derive the name from each sale's billing customer, and match purely on the (last name, first name, date) combination — not on customer id. Consider only sales that have a billing customer on record; exclude anonymous sales with no customer attached. Customers may be missing a first or last name: a missing name part is treated as a value of its own, so two combinations match when all three parts agree (missing matching missing), and combinations containing missing name parts still count toward the total. Report a single count.

comment: Eight half-hour-bucket counts with three dependent/vehicle-count demographic combinations at store 'ese'. Gradeable at scale factor >= 1 (the eval default), where store name 'ese' is present; absent at sf=0.01. Same scale artifact as query 96.
prompt: For store sales at the store named 'ese', to the household recorded on the sale (the sale-time household demographic, not the customer's current one) where the dependent count and vehicle count satisfy one of these three combinations â€” dependent count of 4 with vehicle count no more than 6, dependent count of 2 with vehicle count no more than 4, or dependent count of 0 with vehicle count no more than 2 â€” produce eight separate counts of qualifying sales bucketed by the half-hour of the sold time: 8:30-9:00, 9:00-9:30, 9:30-10:00, 10:00-10:30, 10:30-11:00, 11:00-11:30, 11:30-12:00, and 12:00-12:30. Output one row with the eight counts as separate columns.

For store sales in year 1999, restricted to items in either ('Books','Electronics','Sports') with class in ('computers','stereo','football') or in ('Men','Jewelry','Women') with class in ('shirts','birdal','dresses'), report the total of the unit sale price (the per-unit price the item actually sold for - not its list price, and not the line/extended total) grouped by item category, class, brand, store name, store company name, and month of year. Alongside each row include the average of that monthly total computed across all months of the year for the same (category, brand, store name, store company name). Drop any row whose average is zero. Among the remaining rows, keep only those where the absolute difference between the row's monthly total and the average, divided by the average, is greater than 0.1. Order by (monthly total minus average), then store name, then category, class, brand, store company name, month of year, total, and average; limit 100 rows.

Count web sale lines for ship-to households (the household demographics recorded on the web sale) with a dependent count of 6, on web pages whose character count is between 5000 and 5200, where the sale's sold time falls in the morning hours 8 through 9. Count the same population where the sold time falls in the evening hours 19 through 20. Return a single column only: the morning count divided by the evening count as a decimal ratio (null when the evening count is zero). Order by the ratio; limit 100 rows.

For catalog returns in the 11th month of year 1998, report total net loss by call center, identified by call center code (the stable business identifier), name, and manager. Compute the net loss separately for each combination of the customer's marital status and education, so a call center with returns in more than one such combination appears on more than one row (report only the call center code, name, and manager columns alongside the loss). Restrict to returns by the returning customer whose current home address has a GMT offset of -7, whose household buy potential begins with 'Unknown', and whose marital-status and education combination is either married with 'Unknown' education or widowed with 'Advanced Degree' education. Use the returning customer's own current customer-demographics, household-demographics, and address records. Order by total net loss descending.

For web sales of items from manufacturer 350, sold between 2000-01-27 and 2000-04-26, report the total extended discount amount on lines whose extended discount amount exceeds 1.3 times the average extended discount amount for that same item (across all web sales of that item in the same date window). Output a single total. Order by the total; limit 100 rows.

For each customer, sum an adjusted amount over their store-sale lines that have a matching store return whose reason is 'reason 28'. For each such line: if the return recorded a return quantity, the adjusted amount is (sold quantity minus return quantity) times sales price; otherwise the adjusted amount is sold quantity times sales price. Apply this formula exactly as written: if a line's sold quantity or sales price is not recorded, its adjusted amount is null and must stay null — do not substitute zero — so a customer whose lines are all null has a null sum. Report, per customer, the customer identifier exactly as recorded on the sale line (do not look the customer up in the customer table to translate it) and the summed adjusted amount; ke null customer sks as a null group. Order by the sum then by the customer id, both with nulls first; limit 100 rows.

For web sales whose ship date is between 1999-02-01 and 1999-04-02, shipped to an address in IL, sold through a web site whose company name is 'pri', where - under any situation, not just the prior filters - at least one other line on the same order ships from a different warehouse and no line of that order has a web return, report the count of unique orders, the total extended shipping cost, and the total net profit. Order by the order count; limit 100 rows.

comment: Like query 94 but with a semi-join to web_returns (the order has at least one return). Gradeable at scale factor >= 1 (the eval default), where web_site company name 'pri' is present; absent at sf=0.01.
prompt: For web sales where the order has at least one other line shipping from a different warehouse and at least one web return exists for the order (unconstrained by other conditions), return matching orders from that set where the ship date is between 1999-02-01 and 1999-04-02, shipped to an address in IL, sold through a web site whose company name is 'pri'. Output the count of unique orders, the total extended shipping cost, and the total net profit. Order by the order count; limit 100 rows.

comment: Plain filtered line-item count (sold in the 8 PM hour at/after minute 30, store named 'ese', household dependent count 7). Gradeable at scale factor >= 1 (the eval default), where store name 'ese' is present; absent at sf=0.01.
prompt: Count the store-sale line items that were sold in the 8 PM hour (hour 20 on a 24-hour clock) at or after minute 30, at the store named 'ese', for sales whose recorded household demographics have a dependent count of 7. Order by the count; limit 100 rows.

For sales whose sold date falls in the year 2000, take each unique (customer, item key fk) combination from store sales and each unique (billing customer, item key fk) combination from catalog sales. (Yes, we care about the unique item version, not just the item id). Report three counts: combinations present only in the store set, combinations present only in the catalog set, and combinations present in both. Output a single row with those three counts; limit 100 rows.

For store sales of items in the 'Sports', 'Books', or 'Home' categories sold between 1999-02-22 and 1999-03-24, report for each item its business code, description, category, class, and current price, along with the total extended store sales price for that item, and that item's extended sales price as a percentage of the total extended sales price of all items in its class (the ratio multiplied by 100, between 0 and 100). Report one row per (item code, description, category, class, current price); return every qualifying item and do not cap or limit the number of rows. Order by category, class, item code, description, then the percentage, all with nulls first.

For catalog sales whose ship date falls in the year 2000, and only for sales where the warehouse, shipping mode, and call center are all recorded (non null surrogate keys), report the count of catalog-sale line items (each line item is a unique order + item combination) bucketed by shipping lag (ship date minus sold date, in days) into five columns: 30 days or fewer, 31 to 60 days, 61 to 90 days, 91 to 120 days, and more than 120 days. Report 0 (not null) for any bucket with no matching line items. For each combination of the first 20 characters of the warehouse name, the shipping-mode type, and the lowercased call center name, report the five bucketed counts. Order by the 20-character warehouse name, the shipping-mode type, then the lowercased call center name, all with nulls first; limit 100 rows.
